role_summary: SNCC field secretary; opened the Sunflower County voter-registration project in 1962; managed Fannie Lou Hamer's 1964 MFDP congressional campaign.

photo.alt: Charles McLaurin being honored in Ruleville, Mississippi for the second anniversary of his voter registration work, August 1964

ai_reading: McLaurin's top semantic neighbors are the joint Ladner sisters interview (entry 75, count 267), Lisa Anderson Todd (entry 79), Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96), Juadine Henderson (entry 68), and Reginald Robinson (entry 101). All Mississippi voter-registration figures; the cluster is the tightest Mississippi grouping in the catalog so far, with even the in-bound counts higher than most other entries' top-five cumulative. On the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle axis he sits at -0.83, the deepest Southern reading in the audited subset of the catalog, consistent with his organizing in McComb, Greenwood, Hattiesburg, and Indianola. His Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision position confirms rather than surprises, at +0.49 well toward Tactical Pragmatism: for an organizer whose self-described method was "talk with the people, laugh with them, joke with them" (tactical and relational), the embedding reads his testimony as ground-level field practice, capturing the day-to-day register of the Sunflower County work rather than a movement-level strategic analysis.
biographical_paragraph: In early August 1962, Charles McLaurin, twenty-one years old and out of Jackson, Mississippi, opened SNCC's voter-registration project in Sunflower County and led residents to the Indianola courthouse to try to register [src: 2]. He had come to that work through Medgar Evers, who turned him from the direct-action sit-ins of the Jackson Movement toward registration organizing [src: 2]. By the next month, night riders were firing into Black homes across the county in response [src: 2]. McLaurin worked Indianola and Ruleville alongside SNCC field secretaries Charlie Cobb and Landy McNair; in his own description, his organizing register was to "talk with the people, laugh with them, joke with them" [src: 2]. In 1964 he managed Fannie Lou Hamer's congressional campaign on the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party ticket, and he stayed close to Hamer on political and economic organizing through the 1970s [src: 2]. He later settled in Indianola, where he served as the city's Assistant Public Works Director until retirement [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 17 of the Civil Rights History Project [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Charles McLaurin
interview_snippets.lead_in: On his first day canvassing in Ruleville, a white man with a gun drove the three SNCC organizers to city hall, then released them unharmed. McLaurin read the release as strategy:
interview_snippets.quote: Why didn't we get killed? Why couldn't he run us out of town? Why are we allowed to come back?

interview_snippets.speaker: Lawrence Guyot
interview_snippets.lead_in: Fellow Mississippi organizer Lawrence Guyot places McLaurin in the room the night Fannie Lou Hamer first volunteered to register to vote:
interview_snippets.quote: James Bevel, myself, [Charles] McLaurin, and [James] Forman go to a mass meeting. We open the call for anyone who wants to go down and register. Fannie Lou Hamer raises her hand.

interview_snippets.speaker: Charles McLaurin
interview_snippets.lead_in: On why SNCC chose Sunflower County for a voter-registration project:
interview_snippets.quote: Everything is white, constable, justices of the peace, all the white people and yet blacks are 70% of the whole county.

interview_snippets.speaker: Charles McLaurin
interview_snippets.lead_in: McLaurin on how Amzie Moore had built the NAACP across the Delta plantations before SNCC arrived:
interview_snippets.quote: Amzie Moore organized in NAACP there and he put on his overalls and old clothes and old hat and go out onto the plantations and to register people.

movement_context: Sunflower County in 1962 was the home county of U.S. Senator James O. Eastland (Democrat, chairman of the Senate Judiciary Committee 1956-1978 and the principal southern-Democrat institutional opponent of the civil rights legislation moving through Congress in the early 1960s); the choice of Sunflower County for the August 1962 SNCC voter-registration project Bob Moses and McLaurin opened was a deliberate confrontation with that senatorial fortress [src: 3]. The Indianola courthouse McLaurin led residents to on August 31, 1962 was the same courthouse where SNCC organizer James Bevel had been jailed two weeks earlier and where Fannie Lou Hamer, Annelle Ponder, and June Johnson had been registering since June; the August 31 action made McLaurin the first SNCC field secretary to enter the courthouse with a delegation that day [src: 3]. The Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP) congressional campaign McLaurin managed for Hamer in 1964 was the first congressional campaign mounted by a Black Mississippi candidate since Reconstruction (Hamer ran against incumbent Democrat Jamie L. Whitten in Mississippi's Second Congressional District); the campaign generated the August 1964 Atlantic City Democratic National Convention challenge in which Hamer's televised testimony before the Credentials Committee broke into national prime-time programming when President Johnson called a hastily-arranged White House press conference to displace it [src: 4]. The Atlantic City compromise (two MFDP at-large delegates and a 1968 rule change requiring integrated delegations) was rejected by Hamer and the MFDP delegation; the rejection is the moment Hamer's biographer Kay Mills identified as the turn that began Hamer's later independent organizing through the Freedom Farm Cooperative McLaurin continued to work with her on through the 1970s [src: 4].
legacy_and_reception: McLaurin remains active in Sunflower County and Indianola civil rights commemoration as of the 2010s and 2020s; his work has anchored the annual Fannie Lou Hamer Day commemoration in Ruleville, the public-policy advocacy network that produced the 2014 Federal Highway Administration redesignation of Mississippi Highway 8 through Sunflower County as the Fannie Lou Hamer Highway, and the Sunflower County Civil Rights Organization (SCCRO) institutional record [src: 2, 5]. The principal scholarly account of McLaurin's Sunflower County work is Charles M. Payne's I've Got the Light of Freedom (University of California Press, 1995, second edition 2007), which uses McLaurin's organizing record as the case study for what Payne calls the "organizing tradition" of SNCC field practice as distinguished from the more visible direct-action campaigns [src: 3]. The Kay Mills biography of Hamer (Plume / Penguin, 1994; second edition University of Kentucky Press 2007) and the Maegan Parker Brooks A Voice That Could Stir an Army: Fannie Lou Hamer and the Rhetoric of the Black Freedom Movement (University Press of Mississippi, 2014) both draw on McLaurin's first-person testimony about the 1962-1964 organizing and the 1964 MFDP campaign [src: 6].

gallery.alt: Fannie Lou Hamer at the Democratic National Convention, Atlantic City, August 22, 1964
gallery.caption: Fannie Lou Hamer at the Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City, New Jersey, August 22, 1964, the day she testified before the Credentials Committee about her June 1963 beating in the Winona, Mississippi jail; her televised testimony broke into national prime-time programming when President Lyndon Johnson called a hastily-arranged White House press conference to displace it. McLaurin had managed Hamer's earlier 1964 congressional campaign against incumbent Jamie L. Whitten in Mississippi's Second Congressional District. Photographed by Warren K. Leffler for U.S. News & World Report.
gallery.photographer: Warren K. Leffler (U.S. News & World Report)

gallery.alt: Sunflower County Courthouse in Indianola, Mississippi
gallery.caption: The Sunflower County Courthouse in Indianola, Mississippi. The August 31, 1962 voter-registration action McLaurin led ran to this courthouse; the same courthouse had been the site of the SNCC project's earlier organizing under Fannie Lou Hamer, Annelle Ponder, and June Johnson since June 1962, and was the county-seat fortress of U.S. Senator James O. Eastland's home county.
